apprenticeJournal.title: The Frostbound Ledger
apprenticeJournal.author: Sevrin, Apprentice of the Arcanaeum
apprenticeJournal.subtitle: The First Season at the College — Sun's Dusk to Morning Star · Era of the Fourth

entries.title: The Bridge
entries.body:
I came to Winterhold expecting a city and found a cliff with a handful of houses clinging to it, as if the town itself were afraid to let go. They tell me most of it fell into the sea a lifetime ago. Only the College remained, out across the great stone bridge, standing on its spire of rock above nothing at all.

The gatekeeper — a Breton woman with frost in her voice — would not let me cross until I had shown her a spark of magic in my own hand. My fingers shook with the cold, but a thread of flame answered me, and she stepped aside without a word.

So I have crossed the bridge. Behind me, the wind. Ahead, the light of the Arcanaeum burning in its high windows. I do not think I shall sleep.

entries.title: The Arcanaeum
entries.body:
They gave me a bed in the Hall of Attainment and a single instruction: read. The Arcanaeum is the library that crowns the College, a great curving hall of shelves that seem to lean inward as though listening. The keeper watches over it and does not suffer folded pages, spilled ink, or spoken spells among the stacks.

I have never seen so many books in one place. Whole shelves on Destruction alone; whole shelves on things I did not know had names. I took down a treatise on the schools of magic and read until the candle guttered.

Magic, it seems, is not one art but many, and no mage masters them all. One must choose where to set one's feet. I have not yet chosen. But I have begun.

entries.title: First Frost
entries.body:
My first proper lesson. The tutor set a candle-flame between us and asked me not to snuff it but to still it — to draw the warmth out of the air around it until the flame itself shivered and turned blue.

Destruction, she said, is not only fire. It is the shaping of raw force, and cold is force as surely as heat. To freeze is only to command the heat elsewhere.

I failed a dozen times and put out the candle twice. On the thirteenth, the flame paled and a rime of ice crept across the table toward my hand, and I felt for one heartbeat the whole cold weight of the north pass through me like a held breath. Then it broke, and I was only a tired apprentice in a stone room. But I had done it. Once is enough to know a thing is real.

entries.title: On Being Watched
entries.body:
The townsfolk of Winterhold do not love us. When I crossed to the inn for supper an old man spat in the snow as I passed and named the College the cause of the Collapse — as if we had called the sea up to swallow their homes.

The truth is more troubling than the accusation. No one at the College will say for certain what caused the great wave that took the city. Some blame the eruption far to the south. Some blame the mages who were here before us. The books in the Arcanaeum grow strangely quiet on the matter, and a quiet book is often the loudest kind.

I begin to understand that to study here is to live beside a wound no one will name.

entries.title: The Conjuration Ward
entries.body:
Today I watched a senior mage summon a flame atronach inside a ring of silver set into the floor. It rose out of nothing, a burning figure turning slowly in place, and it fixed its molten eyes on each of us in turn as if choosing.

Conjuration, the tutor warned, is a doorway, and a doorway swings both ways. The bound thing serves only so long as the binding holds and the summoner's will does not falter. She made us recite the closing words until we could say them faster than we could think them.

"The most dangerous mage," she told us, "is not the one who cannot summon. It is the one who forgets how to send back." I have written the closing words on the inside of my wrist in ink. I will not be that mage.

entries.title: Restoration, Which They Mock
entries.body:
There is a fashion among some apprentices to sneer at Restoration — to call it the healer's trade, unworthy of a true mage. I confess I half believed it until this evening.

The tutor drew a blade across her own palm without flinching and let it bleed, then closed her hand and opened it again, whole and clean, the light of the mending still fading from her skin. Then she asked which of us could do the same, and the room went very silent.

To unmake a wound is no lesser art than to make one. He who can only harm is at the mercy of the first wound he cannot answer. I mean to learn this school, whatever the others say. Pride is a poor bandage.

entries.title: The Locked Case
entries.body:
There is a case at the far end of the Arcanaeum, glass and iron, and the books within it are chained. The keeper will not open it, and when I asked what they were, only said: "Books that were opened once already, by someone who is no longer here to regret it."

I have learned since that they concern the older schools — the arts the College no longer teaches openly. Necromancy is forbidden within these walls, and there are other studies darker still that are not even given the dignity of a name in the catalogue.

I understand the reason. I also understand, now, the particular hunger that comes over a scholar when a thing is placed behind glass. That hunger is itself a subject worth studying. I study it, and I keep walking.

entries.title: Aurora
entries.body:
Tonight the sky over the College caught fire without heat. The northern lights — great slow banners of green and violet — hung above the spire and turned the snow every colour at once. The apprentices went up to the walls to watch, and even the sour senior mages came, and for a while no one spoke of the Collapse or the townsfolk or the chained books.

One of the older Altmer told me the lights are the breath of Aetherius seen through the wounds the world was born with — that magicka itself pours down through them, and that the first mages learned their art by watching the sky and trying to answer it.

I do not know if that is true. But I stood in the cold with light running over my hands, and I thought: this is why I crossed the bridge. Not for the power. For this.

entries.title: Alteration and the Weight of Things
entries.body:
Alteration is the strangest school, and the tutor who teaches it is stranger still. He spent our whole lesson insisting that stone is only a habit the world has, and that a sufficiently stubborn mage may persuade it to have a different one.

He hardened his own skin until a hammer rang off his forearm like an anvil. Then he made the floor beneath a chair briefly forget it was solid, and the chair sank to its seat and stuck there.

"Reality," he said, "is an argument, and most people never think to join it." I am not sure I followed all of it. But I lifted a candlestick without touching it, and though it wobbled like a newborn foal, it rose. The world can be argued with. I have written that down twice so I will not forget it.

entries.title: Illusion, and Whom to Trust
entries.body:
The Illusion tutor kept us waiting an hour, then revealed she had been sitting among us the whole time, cloaked from our sight. Half the class had been speaking freely of her, and she let us finish before she let us see her. A cruel lesson, and an effective one.

Illusion works not on the world but on the mind — courage and fear, calm and fury, the very seeing of the eye. It is the gentlest school and the most feared, for a fireball can be seen coming and a whispered false thought cannot.

She made me feel a joy I had no reason for, then took it away, so that I might know the difference between a feeling I had earned and one that had been placed in me. I have not stopped thinking about how rarely, in an ordinary life, we are ever told the difference.

entries.title: The Choice
entries.body:
A season gone, and the keeper asked me today what I mean to study. I have touched every school and mastered none. Fire and frost, mending and warding, the summoned flame, the argued stone, the whispered feeling.

I told her the truth: that I do not wish to choose, that each school seems a window onto the same vast room seen from a different wall. She did not scold me for it. She said the greatest scholars of the Arcanaeum have always been the ones who kept every window open, though it is the longer and colder road.

So that is the road I will walk. I set this ledger upon the shelf now, that whoever comes after me — some frightened apprentice fresh across the bridge — may know that the cold is worth it, and the light is real, and the closing words go on the inside of the wrist. Begin, and keep beginning.
